title: Software for Education
description: A curated guide to essential software tools for educational institutions covering communication, PM, design, collaboration, and learning analytics.
challenges: Limited technology budgets and funding uncertainty â€” educational institutions operate under tight, often annually determined budgets with competing priorities for classroom resources, infrastructure, and staffing, requiring software decisions that maximize educational impact per dollar and leverage free or discounted tiers available through education licensing programs.

Supporting diverse learning modalities â€” modern education must accommodate in-person, fully remote, and hybrid learning environments simultaneously, requiring flexible technology platforms that deliver consistent experiences across physical classrooms, video conferencing, asynchronous learning management systems, and mobile devices without requiring faculty to master multiple interfaces.

Faculty adoption and technology proficiency gaps â€” classroom technology adoption depends on individual instructor willingness and ability to integrate new tools into their teaching practice, requiring platforms that are intuitive enough for technology-hesitant faculty while powerful enough for early adopters, with adequate professional development and support resources.

Data privacy and student information protection â€” educational institutions are subject to FERPA, COPPA, and increasingly state-level student data privacy laws that govern how student records, online behavior data, and personally identifiable information are collected, stored, shared, and protected across the technology ecosystem serving students from pre-K through higher education.

Measuring educational outcomes and technology ROI â€” institutions struggle to connect technology investments to measurable learning outcomes, requiring analytics tools that track engagement, assessment performance, and skill mastery while respecting student privacy, and that can demonstrate to administrators and boards that technology spending translates to improved student achievement.

recommendations.bestFor: Educational institutions and departments that need a flexible all-in-one workspace for curriculum planning, departmental project management, knowledge bases, and collaborative documentation. Notion's versatility makes it uniquely suited for education â€” it serves as a learning management system supplement, a departmental wiki, a curriculum planning tool, and a student project collaboration space all within the same platform. For curriculum development, Notion lets instructional designers create structured course outlines with embedded resources, assignment descriptions, rubrics, and assessment criteria that can be shared with faculty for collaborative refinement. Departments can use Notion for strategic planning â€” tracking accreditation requirements, program reviews, faculty hiring, and budget planning in connected databases with timeline views and assignable tasks. For students, Notion provides a personal knowledge management system where they can take notes, organize research, manage group project tasks, and build portfolios. Notion's Education plan is free for verified educators and students with unlimited pages and blocks, making it accessible for institutions of any budget. The platform's template gallery includes pre-built education templates for lesson planning, grade tracking, and syllabus design.

Educators and students who need an accessible, browser-based design platform for creating presentations, infographics, worksheets, posters, videos, and other visual learning materials without requiring graphic design skills or expensive software licenses. Canva for Education is free for Kâ€“12 teachers and students, providing access to 100+ million stock photos, videos, audio, and graphics, 610,000+ premium templates, and classroom-specific features like assignment workflows, student collaboration, and LMS integration with Google Classroom, Canvas, and Schoology. Teachers use Canva to create visually engaging presentations that replace text-heavy slides, design infographics that help visual learners understand complex concepts, produce interactive worksheets with drag-and-drop activities, and create videos with animations and screen recordings for flipped classroom content. Students use Canva for project-based learning â€” creating reports, posters, digital storytelling projects, and portfolios that build visual communication skills. The platform's accessibility features include alt text for images, high-contrast modes, and screen reader support, ensuring learning materials are inclusive. Canva's brand kits let schools maintain consistent visual identity across all communications, and the print service can produce professionally printed materials for school events.

rows: Google Workspace for Education â€” Free for qualifying institutions with email, unlimited Drive storage, Google Meet with captions and recording, Google Classroom integration, and 200+ education app integrations in a FERPA-compliant platform.
Schools and universities that need a comprehensive, FERPA-compliant collaboration platform with learning management capabilities and the lowest total cost of ownership for education.

Notion â€” All-in-one workspace for curriculum planning, departmental wikis, strategic planning databases, student project collaboration, and knowledge management with free Education plan for verified educators and students.
Educational departments and institutions needing a flexible platform that combines documentation, project management, and knowledge base functionality without the rigidity of traditional PM tools.

Canva for Education â€” Free design platform for Kâ€“12 with 100M+ stock assets, 610K+ templates, classroom assignment workflows, student collaboration, and LMS integration with Google Classroom, Canvas, and Schoology.

Educators who want to create professional-quality visual learning materials â€” presentations, infographics, worksheets, videos â€” without design training or expensive software, plus students building visual communication skills.

Amplitude â€” Event-based product analytics with behavioral cohorts, funnel analysis, and engagement tracking adapted for understanding student behavior in digital learning platforms and online course environments.
Education technology teams and institutional researchers who need granular behavioral analytics to understand how students engage with digital learning tools and identify improvement opportunities.

Todoist â€” Cross-platform task management with natural language input, project organization, recurring tasks for weekly academic rhythms, collaboration for study groups, and Google Calendar/Gmail integration.
Students and educators who need a fast, reliable task management system to track assignments, deadlines, and class preparation across all their devices with minimal friction.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Google Workspace for Education tiers?
faqs.answer: Google Workspace for Education offers four tiers that differ primarily in feature depth, storage limits, and advanced capabilities. Education Fundamentals is free and includes the core tools: Gmail, Google Classroom, Google Meet (100 participants, no recording), Google Drive, Docs, Sheets, Slides, and enterprise-grade security and compliance. This tier is sufficient for most Kâ€“12 schools and many higher education needs. Education Standard costs $3 per student per year and adds enhanced security controls including security center, Vault for eDiscovery and audit, and advanced device management â€” recommended for institutions with compliance requirements or that need to retain and search communication records. Teaching and Learning Upgrade costs $4 per license per month and is designed for instructors rather than all students â€” it adds Google Meet with 250 participants, polling, Q&A, breakout rooms, recording, and originality reports in Classroom. Education Plus costs $5 per student per year and combines everything â€” the advanced security of Standard, the teaching features of T&L Upgrade, plus Meet live streaming for 10,000 viewers within the domain, advanced analytics, and additional Classroom features like rubric-based grading and grade exporting. Most institutions start with Fundamentals and add the Teaching and Learning Upgrade for faculty who need enhanced Meet features for hybrid instruction. The transition between tiers is straightforward since all tiers use the same platform and data, with no data migration required.

faqs.question: How can educational institutions ensure student data privacy when using cloud software?
faqs.answer: Ensuring student data privacy in cloud software requires a systematic approach at both the procurement and operational levels. At procurement, every software vendor must provide a signed Data Privacy Agreement or Business Associate Agreement that specifies exactly what student data they collect, how it is stored and protected, whether it is shared with third parties, and how it is deleted when the relationship ends. Kâ€“12 schools in the US should use the Student Data Privacy Consortium's common framework, which standardizes privacy agreements across states and vendors. At the institutional level, maintain a public inventory of all software tools in use with their privacy certifications, data handling practices, and the legal basis for data collection under FERPA or GDPR. Limit data collection to only what is educationally necessary â€” many apps request access to student location, contacts, or browsing history that serve advertising rather than educational purposes and should be blocked. Implement technical controls like Google Workspace's data regions to ensure student data stays within specified geographic boundaries, restricted sharing settings to prevent accidental exposure of student records, and account provisioning through the institution's identity management system so student access is automatically revoked upon graduation or withdrawal. Train all faculty and staff on privacy-aware technology selection â€” they should know how to evaluate a tool's privacy practices before adopting it and whom to contact when they are unsure.

faqs.question: What are the best practices for implementing a learning management system?
faqs.answer: Successful LMS implementation follows a structured adoption framework that prioritizes faculty readiness, course quality standards, and continuous evaluation. The first step is forming an implementation team that includes instructional designers, IT support, faculty representatives from each department, and student representatives â€” no group should be surprised by the new system. Define course quality standards that specify minimum requirements for every course in the LMS: a syllabus with learning objectives, a weekly schedule with assigned readings and activities, gradebook setup with weighted categories, and at least one communication channel (announcements, discussions, or messages). Provide a course template that faculty can customize rather than starting from blank pages â€” templates dramatically reduce the time to launch and ensure consistency in navigation, terminology, and student experience. Schedule staged rollout: pilot the LMS with 10â€“20 willing faculty across different departments for one semester, gathering feedback and refining processes before institution-wide launch. Provide peer-led training where early-adopter faculty train their colleagues in department-specific contexts rather than generic vendor training â€” faculty trust and learn better from peers who understand their specific teaching challenges. Establish a help desk with LMS-specific expertise and create a knowledge base of common questions and solutions. After launch, regularly review LMS analytics â€” course completion rates, assessment submission times, discussion participation, and grade distributions â€” to identify courses or programs that may need additional support. Plan for the reality that full faculty adoption of LMS features takes 2â€“3 academic years even under optimal conditions.

faqs.question: What technology tools help support students with disabilities?
faqs.answer: Supporting students with disabilities requires a combination of built-in accessibility features in mainstream tools and specialized assistive technologies. Screen readers like JAWS and NVDA remain essential for students with visual impairments, and institutions should ensure all digital content â€” PDFs, videos, LMS pages, and documents â€” meets WCAG 2.1 AA standards with proper heading structures, alt text on images, and descriptive link text. Text-to-speech tools like Read&Write and Kurzweil 3000 help students with reading disabilities, dyslexia, and ADHD by reading text aloud with synchronized highlighting, providing vocabulary support, and offering voice-to-text dictation for writing assignments. Speech recognition tools like Dragon NaturallySpeaking enable students with physical disabilities or writing difficulties to compose by voice. For students with hearing impairments, automatic captioning tools â€” Google Meet's live captions, Zoom's live transcription, or Otter.ai for recorded lectures - have become increasingly accurate and are often sufficient for classroom participation. Note-taking support tools like Glean or Sonocent Audio Notetaker let students with processing or attention difficulties record lectures with synchronized note-taking, audio bookmarking, and automated transcription. The most critical institutional practice is the procurement accessibility review â€” every new technology tool should be evaluated for VPAT (Voluntary Product Accessibility Template) compliance before purchase, and vendors that cannot meet WCAG standards should be excluded. Universal Design for Learning principles should guide all technology decisions, benefiting all students, not just those with identified disabilities.

faqs.question: How should schools and universities approach hybrid learning technology?
faqs.answer: Hybrid learning technology requires an intentional classroom design that serves in-person and remote students equitably, avoiding the common pitfall where remote participants become passive observers. The foundation is a classroom with high-quality audio and video: ceiling-mounted microphones or wireless lapel mics that capture the instructor clearly, a pan-tilt-zoom camera that follows the instructor or shows the whiteboard, and a second camera for a room view so remote students see their peers. The main display should show the remote participants in gallery view or spotlight the current speaker, and a second display can show shared content. The platform choice between Zoom, Google Meet, and Microsoft Teams depends on the institution's ecosystem, but all three support hybrid features â€” breakout rooms that mix in-person and remote students, shared digital whiteboards (Miro, Mural, or Google Jamboard), and polling for real-time engagement checks. Classroom microphones should capture student questions from anywhere in the room, and a teaching assistant or moderator should monitor the chat for remote participant questions and interject them naturally into the discussion. The pedagogical shift is most important: design activities that explicitly require participation from both groups â€” think-pair-share using digital tools, collaborative documents edited simultaneously by remote and in-person students, and structured turn-taking that acknowledges remote participants by name. Institutions should pilot hybrid classrooms with willing faculty, invest in dedicated equipment rather than BYO solutions, and provide technical support staff in the room for the first weeks of each semester.

faqs.question: What should be included in a student's educational technology competency?
faqs.answer: Educational institutions have a responsibility to ensure students graduate with core technology competencies that prepare them for modern workplaces and lifelong learning. At minimum, students should be proficient in collaborative document creation and version management using tools like Google Docs or Office 365 â€” understanding real-time co-authoring, commenting, suggesting mode, and revision history â€” because these are the standard collaborative workflows across most professional environments. Students should be able to create and deliver effective presentations using tools like Google Slides, PowerPoint, or Canva with attention to visual design principles, data visualization, and public speaking with slide support. Data literacy fundamentals include creating and interpreting spreadsheets with formulas, pivot tables, and charts in Google Sheets or Excel, and understanding basic statistical concepts like averages, distributions, and correlations. Students should be experienced with project management tools, whether Asana, Trello, or Notion, to manage group projects with assigned tasks, deadlines, and shared workspaces. Digital citizenship competencies include understanding online privacy settings, recognizing phishing and misinformation, practicing respectful online communication, and knowing how to evaluate source credibility. For more specialized programs, add discipline-specific competencies â€” engineering students need CAD software proficiency, design students need Figma and Adobe Creative Cloud, business students need CRM and ERP exposure, and computer science students need version control with Git and GitHub. These competencies should be mapped across the curriculum so they are introduced early, reinforced in mid-level courses, and demonstrated capstone-level proficiency.

faqs.question: How can schools measure the effectiveness of education technology investments?
faqs.answer: Measuring education technology effectiveness requires a framework that connects technology usage to learning outcomes through intermediary engagement and behavioral metrics. The most rigorous approach uses a logic model: inputs (technology investment, training hours) lead to outputs (adoption rate, usage frequency, feature utilization) which lead to outcomes (student engagement, assessment performance, retention) which lead to impact (graduation rates, career readiness). At the output level, track adoption rate (percentage of faculty and students using the tool weekly), feature utilization (are they using basic features only or advanced interactive capabilities), and consistency of use across courses and departments. Low adoption suggests training or usability issues rather than technology failure. At the outcome level, use LMS analytics to correlate tool usage with engagement â€” students who use interactive features like discussion forums or practice quizzes show higher engagement scores and typically achieve better assessment results. Controlled pilots where some sections use a new tool and others use traditional methods provide the strongest evidence of effectiveness, particularly when students are randomly assigned. For institution-level decisions, analyze retention and completion rates before and after technology implementation, controlling for other variables like enrollment changes and curriculum revisions. The most practical ongoing measurement is a regular technology survey that asks students and faculty about tool usefulness, ease of use, and perceived impact on learning â€” qualitative feedback often reveals issues that usage data misses. The key is to define success metrics before deployment, not after, and to be willing to disinvest in tools that do not demonstrate measurable impact within two academic years.
